title: Community Notes and Prediction Markets: Info-Defense Tools for the 21st Century
body_html:
The information environment is broken in ways that aren't going to be fixed by better journalism or more aggressive content moderation. Both of those solutions require centralized actors making editorial judgments, and the problem with centralized editorial judgment is that it creates an obvious target for capture: whoever controls the editorial filter controls what counts as true. The challenge for information defense is to build mechanisms that help people identify truth without concentrating the power to define truth in any single actor's hands. Vitalik Buterin highlights two existing tools as genuine examples of this working in practice: Community Notes and prediction markets.

Community Notes — Buterin wrote an extended analysis of it in 2023 — is a Wikipedia-style fact-checking layer on X/Twitter where users can add context to tweets. The technically interesting part is the algorithm: rather than surfacing notes that the most people approve of (which would just surface whatever the majority faction endorses), it surfaces notes that are approved by users across the political spectrum. The algorithm explicitly requires cross-partisan consensus. A note that left-leaning users approve and right-leaning users don't, or vice versa, doesn't get shown. Only notes that both sides are willing to endorse make it to wide display.

This is a genuinely clever mechanism design choice. It makes the system structurally resistant to capture by any single political faction. You can't spam Community Notes with your side's narrative because your side's approval alone isn't sufficient — you need the other side's approval too. In practice, this means Community Notes tends to flag things that are clearly, demonstrably wrong in ways that cross partisan lines: mistaken dates, misattributed quotes, missing context that changes a story's meaning completely. It doesn't resolve genuinely contested political questions, which is appropriate — those questions don't have algorithm-discoverable correct answers.

Prediction markets take a different approach to a related problem: not "what is false" but "what will happen." Polymarket and similar platforms aggregate probabilistic beliefs about future events using financial incentives to make participants reveal their true beliefs rather than their preferred narratives. During the OpenAI CEO drama in November 2023, prediction markets gave real-time probability estimates on whether Sam Altman would be reinstated, giving context to people following the story who couldn't tell which moment was decisive. During elections, prediction markets have often beaten poll-based models in accuracy.

Both mechanisms share a structural property Buterin finds essential: they use incentive design rather than authority to select for accuracy. Community Notes doesn't need a trusted editorial board — the cross-partisan consensus algorithm does the work. Prediction markets don't need expert forecasters — the financial stake does the work. This makes them much more resistant to the failure modes of centralized information systems: editorial capture, expertise gatekeeping, and political pressure on curators.

The frontier from here is deployment at scale. Community Notes covers one platform. A version of the algorithm embedded in browsers and covering the full internet rather than a single platform would be qualitatively different. Prediction markets are currently limited by low liquidity on all but the highest-profile events; AI-assisted market participation could dramatically expand coverage. Both tools are early prototypes of what Buterin calls "info defense" — the kind of decentralized truth-finding mechanisms that can help a society reason well without concentrating the power to define reality in any single institution's hands.
